text:
IMPARTIAL NEWS + INTELLIGENT DEBATE

Account

From Marchand mania to Sifan Hassan's incredible feat, here are some of the most memorable moments from a truly remarkable Games

A pulsating 16 days inParisthrew up no shortage of shocks, heartbreak and extraordinaryOlympicperformances.

With the curtain now drawn on a triumph of a Games back on European soil – the first sinceLondon 2012– our team on the ground have picked out some of the most memorable moments after a thrilling fortnight that captured the hearts of the nation both in France and beyond.

Kristen Faulkner

As the road race wound around Montmartre and reached its business end, big stars like Lotte Kopecky and Marianne Vos were poised to strike.

Enter a former venture capitalist from Alaska who only took up cycling seven years ago to rip up the script and become the first American winner of the event in 40 years.

Roje Stona

When world record holder Mykolas Alekna broke his father’s Olympic record in the second round, the men’s discus only seemed to be going one way.

But Stona, who finished sixth at the Commonwealth Games and 19th at the World Championships, uncorked a 70-metre personal best and an Olympic record to stun Stade de France and take gold.

USA women’s water polo

With rapper Flavor Flav as an unlikely hype man, Team USA were big favourites to win a fourth straight Olympic title.

But they lost to Australia in the quarter-final and then conceded in the last minute of their bronze medal match to go down 11-10 to the Netherlands, shockingly missing the podium for the first time since the sport was introduced at Sydney 2000.

France men’s handball

This could have been a glorious coronation for one of the host nation’s most popular sports teams and a golden handshake for the retiring Nikola Karabatic, the greatest handball player of them all.

The side formerly known as ‘Les Experts’ proved anything but as they stank out the group stage and were beaten in the quarter-finals by unfancied Germany, to the horror of a 27,000 crowd in Lille.

Zheng Qinwen

There were plenty of other players ahead of Zheng in the tennis pecking order, including clay queen Iga Swiatek.

Zheng beat Swiatek soundly in the semi-finals and then thrashed Donna Vekic in the final to become the first Chinese player to win Olympic singles gold.

Vinesh Phogat

Phogat was due to become the first Indian to compete in a wrestling final until being disqualified for being marginally over the 50kg weight at the mandatory weigh-in.

The 30-year-old had taken extreme measures to make the weight, from cutting her hair to drawing blood, and was later taken to a polyclinic at the Olympic Village following dehydration caused by the lack of food and fluid. She has since retired.

Su Weide

China’s gymnasts looked all set to win men’s team gold until Su fell twice on his high bar routine, handing Japan glory.

His apology video on Weibo has been viewed more than 400 million times – and he soon turned off comments as the grimly predictable abuse rolled in.

Jordan Chiles

Sticking with gymnastics and social media, Jordan Chiles is taking time off Instagram ‘for the sake of her mental health’ after being ordered to return her bronze medal.

Chiles was originally on the podium in the women’s floor but the medal has now been awarded to Romania’s Ana Barbosu after the sport’s highest court voided the on-court appeal by Chiles’ coach that took the American into third.

Carolina Marin

The Spanish badminton star won gold in Rio but missed Tokyo through a knee injury – which returned at the worst possible moment.

Marin was 21-14 10-8 up against He Bing Jiao when she ruptured her cruciate ligament and left the arena in tears. Her sympathetic Chinese opponent wore a Spanish pin badge on the podium after being beaten in the final.

Emma Wilson

The British sailor claimed she was “done with this sport” after becoming a victim of windsurfing’s quirky format.

Having won eight of the 14 qualifying races, she advanced straight through to the final – where scores are wiped clean – while her rivals raced again in semis.

Wilson felt this gave them an unfair advantage after coming home third and said: “They need to think about people’s mental health, it’s not okay to be put in that position every time.”

Leon Marchand

From the moment he streaked clear of the field in the 400m individual medley on the second night of swimming, it was clear that these Games would belong to Marchand.

The Frenchman ended up winning four golds in all, including an evening where he won both the 200m butterfly and 200m breaststroke in front of probably the loudest crowd at any Paris venue, beating the defending Olympic champions in each race.

Sydney McLaughlin-Levrone

If you want to know just how dominant McLaughlin-Levrone is, consider that her 400m hurdles time of 50.37 would have been ninth in the semi-finals of the 400m flat.

She is not just the fastest in the world, she was a second and a half faster than anyone else. The Netherlands’ Femke Bol is a generational athlete in her own right, and she could not get close.

Harrie Lavreysen

For the first time in 20 years, Great Britain did not finish top of the medals table in track cycling. That honour instead belonged to the Netherlands, and it was all down to one man.

Lavreysen combined with Jeffrey Hoogland and Roy van den Berg to win team sprint gold before going on to take the individual sprint and keirin titles, meaning he joins Jason Kenny and Chris Hoy as the only people to have won all three sprint golds at the same Games.

Sifan Hassan

The Olympics is all about remarkable feats but even by those incredibly high standards, Hassan stands out.

By winning the Olympic marathon, she completed an unprecedented treble, finishing on the podium of the 5000m and 10000m. Add in the fact that she also has an Olympic medal in the 1500m from Tokyo and she has a case for being one of the most versatile distant runners of all time.

Jessica Fox

Canoe slalom is an unpredictable sport at the best of times, an unfortunate current here can derail an Olympic dream.

And yet Australia’s Fox somehow seems to rise above. After winning C1 gold in Tokyo, she was even better in the country of her birth as she took gold in both C1 and K1.

Impartial news + intelligent debate

title: The 15 best performances and biggest surprises of the Paris Olympics
author: Sportsbeat
description: From Marchand mania to Sifan Hassan's incredible feat, here are some of the most memorable moments from a truly remarkable Games
